papers.title: Discovery of two new hypervelocity stars from the LAMOST spectroscopic surveys
papers.author_names: Yang Huang Xiaowei Liu Huawei Zhang Bingqiu Chen Maosheng Xiang Chun Wang Haibo Yuan Zhijia Tian Yinbi Li Bo Wang

abstract.text: We report the discovery of two new unbound hypervelocity stars (HVSs) from the LAMOST spectroscopic surveys. They are respectively a B2V type star of ~ 7 M$_{\\\\rm \\\\odot}$ with a Galactic rest-frame radial velocity of 502 km/s at a Galactocentric radius of ~ 21 kpc and a B7V type star of ~ 4 M$_{\\\\rm \\\\odot}$ with a Galactic rest-frame radial velocity of 408 km/s at a Galactocentric radius of ~ 30 kpc. The origins of the two HVSs are not clear given their currently poorly measured proper motions. However, the future data releases of Gaia should provide proper motion measurements accurate enough to solve this problem. The ongoing LAMOST spectroscopic surveys are expected to yield more HVSs to form a statistical sample, providing vital constraint on understanding the nature of HVSs and their ejection mechanisms.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A systematic search of the LAMOST spectroscopic survey reveals two new unbound hypervelocity stars (HVSs): a B2V star (~7 M⊙) with 502 km s⁻¹ at 21 kpc and a B7V star (~4 M⊙) with 408 km s⁻¹ at 30 kpc. Their origins remain ambiguous due to unresolved proper motions, but future Gaia data will enable 3D kinematic constraints to distinguish between Galactic center ejection and alternative models like tidal debris or binary interactions. The growing HVS sample from LAMOST enhances statistical power to constrain Galactic mass distribution and ejection mechanisms.

source_fields.title: Discovery of two new hypervelocity stars from the LAMOST spectroscopic surveys

source_fields.sections_text: 4. Summary: A systematic search using LAMOST data identifies three high-velocity stars, all associated with the Galactic center and suggesting a GC origin, with two requiring blue straggler status to survive their flight times, highlighting the need for proper motion measurements to confirm their origins. 1. Introduction: This paper presents a novel discovery of three new hypervelocity stars using the latest LAMOST data, significantly expanding the statistical sample of such objects and providing critical new constraints on the origin and dynamics of hypervelocity stars beyond the traditional Galactic center ejection model. 3. Results and discussion: The three LAMOST-discovered hypervelocity stars (LAMOST-HVS1, HVS2, and HVS3) exhibit Galactic rest-frame radial velocities of 473.5 km s⁻¹, 502.3 km s⁻¹, and 408.3 km s⁻¹, respectively, with Galactocentric radii of 20.1–20.9 kpc and distances of 13.9–22.2 kpc, consistent with their B-type spectral classifications and confirming their origin as hypervelocity stars likely ejected from the Galactic center. 2. Data and target selection: This section describes the use of LAMOST spectroscopic data to search for both old, low-mass and young, massive high-velocity stars, with results constraining the mechanisms of HVS ejection and suggesting a top-heavy initial mass function for their parent populations.

abstract: We report the discovery of two new unbound hypervelocity stars (HVSs) from the LAMOST spectroscopic surveys.
title: Discovery of two new hypervelocity stars from the LAMOST spectroscopic surveys

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly report discovery of two new hypervelocity stars from LAMOST survey with specific stellar parameters (types, masses, radial velocities, distances). Paper presents actual observational data for specific objects.

papers.title: The Origin of B-Type Runaway Stars: Non-LTE Abundances as a Diagnostic
papers.author_names: Catherine M. McEvoy Philip L. Dufton Jonathan V. Smoker David L. Lambert Francis P. Keenan Fabian R. Schneider Willem-Jan de Witt

abstract.text: There are two accepted mechanisms to explain the origin of runaway OB-type stars: the Binary Supernova Scenario (BSS), and the Cluster Ejection Scenario (CES). In the former, a supernova explosion within a close binary ejects the secondary star, while in the latter close multi-body interactions in a dense cluster cause one or more of the stars to be ejected from the region at high velocity. Both mechanisms have the potential to affect the surface composition of the runaway star. TLUSTY non-LTE model atmosphere calculations have been used to determine atmospheric parameters and carbon, nitrogen, magnesium and silicon abundances for a sample of B-type runaways. These same analytical tools were used by Hunter et al. (2009) for their analysis of 50 B-type open cluster Galactic stars (i.e. non-runaways). Effective temperatures were deduced using the silicon-ionization balance technique, surface gravities from Balmer line profiles and microturbulent velocities derived using the Si spectrum. The runaways show no obvious abundance anomalies when compared with stars in the open clusters. The runaways do show a spread in composition which almost certainly reflects the Galactic abundance gradient and a range in the birthplaces of the runaways in the Galactic disk. Since the observed Galactic abundance gradients of C, N, Mg and Si are of a similar magnitude, the abundance ratios (e.g., N/Mg) are, as obtained, essentially uniform across the sample.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This study conducts the first non-LTE abundance analysis of carbon, nitrogen, magnesium, and silicon in a sample of B-type runaway stars, comparing them to non-runaway B stars in Galactic open clusters. It finds no significant chemical anomalies (e.g., N/Mg or C/Si ratios) that would distinguish runaway stars from cluster stars, suggesting that neither the binary supernova scenario (BSS) nor the cluster ejection scenario (CES) leaves a unique chemical fingerprint; instead, observed compositional spreads reflect Galactic abundance gradients and birthplace variations, implying that chemical diagnostics alone cannot reliably distinguish between the two origin mechanisms.

source_fields.title: The Origin of B-Type Runaway Stars: Non-LTE Abundances as a Diagnostic

source_fields.sections_text: 1. Introduction: This paper presents the first non-LTE analysis of C, N, Mg, and Si abundances in runaway B stars to detect chemical signatures distinguishing binary supernova and cluster ejection origins, addressing the problem of determining the formation history of halo OB main sequence stars. 3. Observations: High-resolution optical spectra were obtained from 26 FEROS, 12 UVES, and 14 McDonald observations over 16 months, with data reduced and quality-controlled to enable accurate abundance and atmospheric parameter analysis of B-type runaway stars. 4. Method of analysis: The paper proposes a method using non-LTE model atmosphere grids from TLUSTY and SYNSPEC to derive atmospheric parameters and chemical abundances for B-type stars, assuming plane-parallel atmospheres and normal helium-to-hydrogen ratios, with baseline abundances derived from Milky Way B-type stars and validated through comparisons with wind models showing good agreement for most stellar parameters. 7. Concluding remarks: The lack of abundance anomalies in runaway B stars suggests neither binary star interactions nor core collapse supernovae are responsible for their ejection, with observed elemental variations likely due to Galactic abundance gradients and birthplace distances, while future binary searches and expanded non-LTE analyses of elements like O and S may help distinguish between the leading runaway formation mechanisms. 2. Selection of Runaway B stars: Runaway B stars in the sample were selected based on high Galactic latitude, large vertical distance from the plane, high peculiar velocities, or prior identification as ejected from the disk, with most having ejection velocities below 400 km s⁻¹ and not escaping the Galaxy, primarily drawn from Silva & Napiwotzki and Tetzlaff et al. catalogues. 5. Ejection mechanisms and abundances: This section highlights that runaway B stars formed via the BSS and CES scenarios show distinct abundance patterns, particularly enrichments in elements like Mg and Si, which may indicate supernova contamination, while initial composition gradients and observational limitations complicate definitive conclusions about their chemical origins. 6. Ejection mechanisms: rotational and radial velocities: The rotational velocity distribution of runaway B stars resembles that of young stellar associations, suggesting the collisional ejection scenario (CES) is the dominant mechanism, while the lack of a correlation between ejection and rotational velocity undermines the binary separation scenario (BSS) as a primary source of runaways.

title: The Origin of B-Type Runaway Stars: Non-LTE Abundances as a Diagnostic

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper analyzes a sample of B-type runaway stars using non-LTE abundance analysis. Abstract mentions 'sample of B-type runaways' and compares them to open cluster stars, indicating object-level observational data is being analyzed.
